sentences.text: He, these doctors, the German doctor, Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH], his friend had connections with a Polish man who used to-- I think he stole those blanks from the office.
And he filled them out the way you want to appear, your name there and everything.
You paid him.
I forgot how much.
And he would come to the woods.
See, the Germans when they made fingerprints, they had a special black like tar or something.
But he didn't have any of this.
He brought a cushion.
And I would, put both fingers like this in it, and like this, and put it on it.
And like this, like this, and put it on it.
Now, you had have a non-retouched picture of you with-- en face-- meaning 3/4 of a face.
And not smiling, not like this.
But I didn't have it.
I couldn't move it around to get one.
So I attached that which I have.
It was entirely the opposite than was needed.
But that the best I could do.
I needed to have a picture.
And it was folded in three.
But outside looked like a-- I said, Kennkarte.
And whenever I had to show it, they didn't open it.
They looked at the outside, and that satisfied them.
If they looked, they would know.
But then one day I was in the town, walking on the street in that [NON-ENGLISH].
And somebody-- and I looked around.
And the town is empty.
What did it mean?
It meant that they were catching people from the street to send to Germany for labor work, for compulsory labor.
I said to myself-- and I started to run as fast as I could.
And I think, I think I came to this door-- [WHISPERS]-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Well, I came to where I work with Mimi.
And I was breathing very hard because I was running.
And I made believe that nothing happened.
I had to hide my face.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Now, I forgot to tell you when I was with this woman there, with the baby Anthony, in that estate.
I always watched through the window, because I expected to have new if my sister is alive or not.
And I was waiting for the mailman.
And I saw the head of the-- of the village, whatever you called him.
I saw him come with the German, like this, and said, I got news that you have Jews hidden here.
And if I find out, that what you'll get.
No, I don't have Jews.
We better find out.
And he came to the estate And it was March 19, I think.
And I was called.
And I was standing near the door by the wall.
And the door opened.
And the Germans came in.
And he said, you have Jews hidden here.
And I heard what's going on.
I quickly-- and that was in the dining room.
I quickly went into the baby's room-- meaning the grandmother's bedroom-- bedroom.
And I took this baby and I put it across.
He was sleeping, as if I take care of the baby.
And I felt hot and cold.
Hot and cold, the blood was coming into the face in a way, like this.
And he came, the father of the baby, opened the door and said, somebody's accusing that I have Jews here.
Do you have a Kenncarte?
I said, yes.
And I handed to him And they were satisfied not to open it.
And they gave it back to me.
When they left, it was noontime.
We were sitting down to eat.
And believe it or not, I think it was some kind of a green pea soup.
And believe it or not, I take a spoon of this into my mouth.
It doesn't go down.
The throat locked up.
I couldn't swallow.
And I'm just sitting like this, not looking in anybody's-- normally we had conversation at the table and everything.
This time everybody's quiet, scared to death even to open their mouth to talk.
And I cannot even smile.
And I am trying.
I don't want them to see.
But I'm struggling to have that spoon of something to go down
After?
[NON-ENGLISH]?
Here is the story.
She was pregnant.
And she was going to have twins, the doctor said.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Wait a minute.
I was in Warsaw that time, right?
Well, we've sort of moved along.
Oh, did I tell you, they took me to the front?
They had placards on the street to register as a nurse.
And when I registered, at that time I still was in the sanitarium for TB children.
And one day, here is something interesting.
When I was there, came a soldier [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and took me.
You see what happened.
We had national Germans, and the Volksdeutsche.
Volksdeutsche are those Germans who are born in Poland.
And when the Germans came, they declared themselves that they are Germans.
So they ran off with the German military when they moved.
But some didn't make it.
So the Polish caught them and they took them to jail as collaborators with the Germans.
And they came for me as a nurse to check them.
Is something wrong with them physically?
And I looked at them.
And one was making believe that he is sick.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I said, don't play the role.
There's nothing wrong with you.
And I said to those who brought me there, it's nothing wrong with them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they brought me back to the sanitarium.
Now, next to-- not far from us was a Soviet-- Soviet unit, a military unit.
And we, in that sanitarium, we had a-- a horse.
We had no feed for the horse.
We had a pig.
We had no feed for the pig.
We had a dog.
We had nothing to feed anybody, even for ourselves.
We were all hungry, all.
When I saw their hides hanging on the fence, I thought I'll do something about it.
And the manager of the sanitarium was a woman.
And she said to me, now, you speak Russian.
What I need is for you to go to them and ask them to help us out in some way.
The children are TB children.
We'll have no food for them, and for ourselves, and most-- and for the-- and for the animals-- most of all the children.
I went there, and I said to them, I saw you have these hides hanging.
What do you do with the blood?
He said it runs to the ground.
And I said-- and I could use it.
I'll bring a pail, and you will drain that blood into the pail and keep it for me.
We'll use it.
What do you do with the intestines and the insides?
We bury them.
I say, please save it for me.
We'll use them.
And I went.
There was a Barbara, a girl, working there where I worked.
And I said, Barbara, you come with me.
We went.
And they drained that blood into that pail.
And we were walking through the woods with she holding one side and I the other.
And we brought it there.
And we spilled it into-- we put it in a big pan.
And we put it in the oven.
And it became like liver.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, we were surrounded by woods.
And I used to know how to tell which mushrooms are not poisonous.
I would get up 3:00 in the morning and run to the woods before anybody beats me.
And I would take these mushrooms.
And I would clean them.
Now, when I went to the Russians. i said, what I would need, I need a little pepper and a little salt, and a little-- what do you call it?
An onion.
And I would make a stew out of all this.
And when the stew was a ready, I went to the lieutenant, Mikhail, which is Michael.
And I said now I want to invite you.
And I had a tiny bit of a room, maybe this big.
And I want you to see what I did with what you throw away.
What I did.
I cleaned these intestines.
The little ones I ground up.
Yes.
And they were very glad.
And the Americans gave them to the Soviet military.
These, they call it [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a tin can with stew in it.
And he gave me a can of this stew.
And we became big friends.
Would it be-- it was-- It was before I went to Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Germany as a victorious.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Would it be '43?
Maybe.
Yes, I did go by volunteering.
I got a call to go to a point in a different city.
And I walked from city to city on foot until I got there.
The name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And they-- In that sanitarium was a night watchman, at night.
His wife was supervising the kitchen.
She was a school teacher, he was a school principal in normal time.
And he and I were mobilized.
So we walked together.
He was older.
I was young.
And we're walking.
And I look at him.
Being a nurse, I look at him.
And he's short of breath.
The lips become blue.
And I took it upon myself, and say, it is my fault.
And there were rocks on the road.
And I said, I don't feel good.
Let's sit down.
I was afraid that he would collapse.
And then we took walking.
We came there.
Never forgot this part.
And we had to stay overnight in a barn, or something, with a-- on straw.
And the fleas, God help me.
The fleas were eating us.
And we stayed overnight.
And next morning, they assigned us as to where we have to go.
So I went where I went.
And he--
They, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think they took us in a train.
I think.
And I got news that he died.
He couldn't go through with it.
We were going west-bound to Germany.
Myself, there were other people.
And we came to a city, Poznan in German Posen.
What I saw there, it's unforgettable.
You see, the Germans, what they did [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they filled up the toilets running over.
And-- and in the corner was a dead man, a dead soldier standing there.
It's macabristic.
Then when we were going in the train, and the Poles were very angry with them-- which I never blamed them.
We were going-- in the ditches along side the railroad.
You know, where the-- somebody put a German upside down on his head with the feet up.
And I said to my sister, look, funny.
And she said, oh, don't make fun.
He's dead.
Yes, but then--
Luba.
Then, when-- you see, they put me in the First Polish Army.
We were the first to go west.
It was north.
And she found herself going in the south.
By the time when we were going, the war was still on.
When they were coming, this part, was already-- the war was over.
No, that time I didn't care.
I mean, I didn't run around to advertise.
But by that time, I had no reason to fear, to hide.
I may-- as I said, I didn't run around to advertise it.
Here is the story.
I was, my heart wasn't good.
And they'd decided-- we're still in Warsaw-- and-- and they decide, they put me like you called on a commission.
They took a EKG.
And they found that I need some kind of treatment, or attention, or whatever.
So the best thing to do, they sent me to a sanatorium to take care of me.
When I came there, I said to the lieutenant there, I need to have a room downstairs.
Because I cannot make steps.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well then, it was a dance in town.
And that evening he came to ask me as a partner to his-- to the dance.
When I came there, and when the music played and I started to do the polka.
And he said, and he want-- and you wanted the room downstairs?
[LAUGHS] Now this is before you were on the train to Germany?
No, when we came-- that was later-- when we came to Germany, Bad Morlen.
It was a little town where the soldiers would throw a grenade into the water and the fish would come up.
And They would scoop them.
Now, there were a lot of Soviet units.
We would meet on the street, but we didn't talk.
They going their way.
I'm going my way.
It was, I think, in [NON-ENGLISH], the name of the town.
When we came there, the glass in the windows wasn't there.
The locks on the door wasn't there.
So I put a blanket on the window.
I put the sofa against the door.
So when somebody would want to come in, I would hear the sofa moving.
Now there was a girl on the other end, a nurse, a Soviet nurse, [NON-ENGLISH].
And she-- number one, first I told you about that German woman.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was very afraid of the Soviets.
She didn't know how to speak.
She was very pretty.
And she asked me to stay with me.
I said, OK.
And when the sofa was moving, she was scared to death.
And I said, who is there?
Don't you dare.
I'll go to your manager, to your commandant, and you'll see what you'll get.
And I stopped them.
She was scared.
But the language helps.
It was [NON-ENGLISH], Warsaw-- in Otwock.
Some miles alongside-- in the woods, that sanatorium.
When I was in the sanatorium, I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they started to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when I was in the sanatorium, everybody was hungry.
We had there a-- a orthopedic doctor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had there the secretary of that establishment and everything.
In that time it started to be the new city government.
And we had a permission to go to the field and dig out potatoes and whatever, and take it with us.
Now, we were coupling up-- like I coupled up with the secretary, and we were digging.
And we put them there.
And the city came.
And I thought the more you dig, the more you take.
It wasn't so.
They weighed it.
They weighed it.
This much you take and no more.
I remember, when we were working with that hoe, a hoe on the shoulder, including the orthopedic doctor, [LAUGHING] and the secretary, and everybody.
Now when we were digging out aboard-- across from the river, there were snipers.
And we were standing upright.
And they snipe, you fall to the ground.
That's how we were.
I stayed there in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] till '45.
I worked there.
I worked there.
When I was in-- what was the place?
In Germany, yet.
What happened?
There were two Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fliers.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You know, military.
The Germans shot them down or something.
And they put them into jail.
What happened to me was this.
When these boys-- and somewhat I don't know how, through a miracle.
I remember, my mother had there, in the east coast, three brothers and three sisters.
And for some reason, I remembered one address into Baltimore.
My aunt lived there.
And these boys were living-- they had needed some attention.
So they went to the-- to get attention in that place.
And Tanya, this nurse, came running and said, come, come.
I have something for you.
There are these two boys, they are Americans.
You can talk to them.
I went and I said, I'll give to you and to you an address to my aunt.
And let her know that my sister and I, that we survived.
So they went.
And before you know, we started to correspond.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And when I was back in Warsaw-- oh, I didn't  tell you from [NON-ENGLISH]-- a city by name of-- [INAUDIBLE].
Polish and German, they call it [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
What happened?
On the way back-- it's a-- you call it garrison.
And we call it [NON-ENGLISH].
There was a garrison hospital.
And I was to be there.
And there was a Soviet [NON-ENGLISH].
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was the kind that he had to sleep with every girl.
He was in charge.
But there was also a Polish doctor that he knew me.
And they were like slaves.
They couldn't go to the street.
They had no permission.
But there was a Polish doctor from Warsaw.
I knew him from before the war.
So he gave me a permission, a paper, and I could leave the gate.
And I could go to the street.
And I didn't know anything of anything.
And I met there in the street a Jewish man.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I told him as to who I am.
